summary: I was born in Karachi, Pakistan, and although I left when I was just six months old, it remains a significant part of my identity. Karachi is where my roots lie, and it connects me to my Pakistani heritage through my mother and extended family. Even though I grew up mostly in America, the traditions from Karachi influenced my upbringing, especially through my mother's efforts to maintain cultural practices.

Pakistan is the country where my family originates, and it plays a big role in the cultural traditions my mother tries to uphold. My father, though adopted into an American family, still keeps close contact with relatives in Pakistan, which helps maintain a connection to our roots. Growing up, I struggled with balancing my Pakistani identity with my American upbringing, especially because my mother wanted me to follow Pakistani customs that sometimes conflicted with my life in the United States.

I moved to the United States of America with my mother when I was just six months old to be with my father, who was adopted into an American family. Growing up in the U.S., I had a fairly normal American childhood and was able to express myself freely until my mother started enforcing Pakistani dress traditions around fourth grade. I identify as American but still acknowledge my Pakistani roots, and I hope to raise my future children with an understanding of both cultures without forcing traditions on them.
